results.possibleIdentity: Richard Serra (1938–2024) was a prominent American artist and sculptor primarily known for his large-scale, site-specific abstract sculptures made of industrial materials like steel, lead, and rubber. After moving to New York City in 1966, he became a key figure in the Postminimalism and Process Art movements. During the late 1960s and early 1970s, Serra collaborated with the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization, notably participating in an E.A.T. project where his work was featured in a series of programs broadcast on cable television via Automation House in New York. Additionally, he was a participant in the Los Angeles County Museum of Art's (LACMA) Art and Technology Program (1967–1971), which shared E.A.T.'s goal of fostering collaborations between artists and industry. His early career was marked by experimental film and video works such as 'Hand Catching Lead' (1968) and his 'Splash' series (1968–1970), where he poured molten lead into the corners of galleries. He is widely recognized for his later monumental steel works and the controversial public sculpture 'Tilted Arc' (1981).
